glossaries: Glossary of arithmetic and diophantine geometry

entries.definition: The abc conjecture of Masser and Oesterlé attempts to state as much as possible about repeated prime factors in an equation a + b = c. For example 3 + 125 = 128 but the prime powers here are exceptional.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of arithmetic and diophantine geometry

entries.definition: The abc conjecture says that for all ε > 0, there are only finitely many coprime positive integers a, b, and c satisfying a+b=c such that the product of the distinct prime factors of abc raised to the power of 1+ε is less than c.

glossaries: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: An abelian Lie group is a Lie group that is an abelian group.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: An abelian variety is a complete group variety. For example, consider the complex variety $\mathbb{C}^n/\mathbb{Z}^{2n} $ or an elliptic curve $E$ over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$.
A category is abelian if it has a zero object, it has all pullbacks and pushouts, and all monomorphisms and epimorphisms are normal.
Synonymous with "commutative"; e.g., an abelian Banach algebra means a commutative Banach algebra.
An archaic name for the symplectic group.
A group (G, •) is abelian if • is commutative, i.e. g • h = h • g for all g, h ∈ G. Likewise, a group is nonabelian if this relation fails to hold for any pair g, h ∈ G.

A fixed choice of something in projective space, used to construct some other geometry from projective geometry. For example, choosing a plane, called the absolute plane, of projective space can be used to make its complement into a copy of affine space. Choosing a suitable conic or polarity, called the Cayley absolute, absolute conic or absolute polarity, in the absolute plane provides the means to put a metric on affine space so that it becomes a metric space.
The absolute invariant is essentially the j-invariant of an elliptic curve.
A statement is called absolute if its truth in some model implies its truth in certain related models
Given a cardinal number κ, an object X in a category is κ-accessible (or κ-compact or κ-presentable) if $\operatorname{Hom}(X, -)$ commutes with κ-filtered colimits.
See $T_1$.

A graph is acyclic if it has no cycles. An undirected acyclic graph is the same thing as a forest. An acyclic directed graph, which is a digraph without directed cycles, is often called a directed acyclic graph, especially in computer science.
A binary relation is acyclic if it contains no "cycles": equivalently, its transitive closure is antisymmetric.
John Frank Adams.
Adams operations.

glossaries: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: An adjoint representation of a Lie group: ::$\operatorname{Ad} : G \to \operatorname{GL}(\mathfrak g)$ :such that $\operatorname{Ad}(g)$ is the differential at the identity element of the conjugation $c_g : G \to G, x \mapsto g x g^{-1}$.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: If C is a curve, an adjoint of C is a curve such that any point of C of multiplicity r has multiplicity at least r–1 on the adjoint. Sometimes the multiple points of C are required to be ordinary, and if theis condition is not satisfied the term "sub-adjoint" is used.

The adjoint of a bounded linear operator $T: H_1 \to H_2$ between Hilbert spaces is the bounded linear operator $T^* : H_2 \to H_1$ such that $\langle Tx, y \rangle = \langle x, T^* y \rangle$ for each $x \in H_1, y \in H_2$.
See Galois connection.

The adjoint representation of a Lie group G is the representation given by the adjoint action of G on the Lie algebra of G (an adjoint action is obtained, roughly, by differentiating a conjugation action.)
A representation of a real reductive group is called admissible if (1) a maximal compact subgroup K acts as unitary operators and (2) each irreducible representation of K has finite multiplicity.
An admissible set is a model of Kripke–Platek set theory, and an admissible ordinal is an ordinal α such that Lα is an admissible set

glossaries: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: An affine Lie algebra is a particular type of Kac–Moody algebra.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: Affine space is roughly a vector space where one has forgotten which point is the origin
Affine space is roughly a vector space where one has forgotten which point is the origin.

The topology of a space X is an Alexandrov topology (or is finitely generated) if arbitrary intersections of open sets in X are open, or equivalently, if arbitrary unions of closed sets are closed, or, again equivalently, if the open sets are the upper sets of a poset.
For a preordered set P, any upper set O is Alexandrov-open. Inversely, a topology is Alexandrov if any intersection of open sets is open.
Algebraic geometry is a branch of mathematics that studies solutions to algebraic equations.
a branch that combines techniques from abstract algebra with the language and problems of geometry. Fundamentally, it studies algebraic varieties.
The part of number theory devoted to the use of algebraic methods, mainly those of commutative algebra, for the study of number fields and their rings of integers.
Algebraic number theory

In a graph with a matching, an alternating path is a path whose edges alternate between matched and unmatched edges. An alternating cycle is, similarly, a cycle whose edges alternate between matched and unmatched edges. An augmenting path is an alternating path that starts and ends at unsaturated vertices. A larger matching can be found as the symmetric difference of the matching and the augmenting path; a matching is maximum if and only if it has no augmenting path.
The alternating square of a representation V is a subrepresentation $\operatorname{Alt}^2(V)$ of the second tensor power $V^{\otimes 2}=V\otimes V$.

glossaries: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: An analytic subgroup
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: The analytic spread of an ideal of a local ring is the Krull dimension of the fiber at the special point of the local ring of the Rees algebra of the ideal.
An analytic set is the continuous image of a Polish space. (This is not the same as an analytical set)
part of analysis that studies how well functions can be approximated by simpler ones (such as polynomials or trigonometric polynomials)
Analytic number theory
The annihilator of a subset of a module is the ideal of elements whose product with any element of the subset is 0.
An annihilator is a differential operator representing an element of a Lie algebra, so that invariants of a group are killed by the annihilators.
The annihilator of a left $R$-module $M$ is the set $\textrm{Ann}(M) := \{ r \in R ~|~ rm = 0 \, \forall m \in M \}$ . It is a (two-sided) ideal of $R$.
The annulus theorem.
A ring-shaped object, a region bounded by two concentric circles.

In a directed acyclic graph, a subset S of vertices that are pairwise incomparable, i.e., for any $x \leq y $ in S, there is no directed path from x to y or from y to x. Inspired by the notion of antichains in partially ordered sets.

An antichain is a poset in which no two elements are comparable, i.e., there are no two distinct elements x and y such that x ≤ y. In other words, the order relation of an antichain is just the identity relation.
An antichain is a set of pairwise incompatible elements of a poset
Algebraic geometry over the compactification of Spec of the ring of rational integers $\mathbb{Z}$. See Arakelov geometry.
also known as Arakelov theory

glossaries: Glossary of arithmetic and diophantine geometry

entries.definition: an approach to Diophantine geometry used to study Diophantine equations in higher dimensions (using techniques from algebraic geometry). It is named after Suren Arakelov.
Arakelov theory is an approach to arithmetic geometry that explicitly includes the 'infinite primes'.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of arithmetic and diophantine geometry

entries.definition: The arithmetic scheme is a scheme over $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbf{Z})$, the prime spectrum of the ring of rational integers.
Also known as elementary arithmetic, the methods and rules for computing with addition, subtraction, multiplication and division of numbers.
The ordinal arithmetic is arithmetic on ordinal numbers
The arithmetic genus of a projective variety X of dimension r is $(-1)^r (\chi(\mathcal{O}_X) - 1)$.
The arithmetic genus of a variety is a variation of the Euler characteristic of the trivial line bundle; see Hodge number.

glossaries: Glossary of arithmetic and diophantine geometry

entries.definition: The use of algebraic geometry and more specially scheme theory for solving problems of number theory.

Arithmetic geometry can be defined as the study of schemes of finite type over the spectrum of the ring of integers. It has also been defined as the application of the techniques of algebraic geometry to problems in number theory.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of arithmetic and diophantine geometry

entries.definition: Emil Artin
The Artin conjecture says Artin's L function is entire (holomorphic on the entire complex plane).
Emil Artin.
0-dimensional and Noetherian. The definition applies both to a scheme and a ring.
Emil Artin
An Artinian module is a module in which every decreasing chain of submodules becomes stationary after finitely many steps.

A left Artinian ring is a ring satisfying the descending chain condition for left ideals; a right Artinian ring is one satisfying the descending chain condition for right ideals. If a ring is both left and right Artinian, it is called Artinian. Artinian rings are Noetherian rings.
An associated curve is the image of a projective curve in a Grassmannian, given by taking the tangent lines, or osculating planes, and so on.
An associated prime of a module M over a ring R is a prime ideal p such that M has a submodule isomorphic to R/p.
An atom in a poset P with least element 0, is an element that is minimal among all elements that are unequal to 0.
An urelement, something that is not a set but allowed to be an element of a set
An atomic poset P with least element 0 is one in which, for every non-zero element x of P, there is an atom a of P with a ≤ x.
An atomic formula (in set theory) is one of the form x=y or x∈y

glossaries: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: An automorphism of a Lie algebra is a linear automorphism preserving the bracket.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: A graph automorphism is a symmetry of a graph, an isomorphism from the graph to itself.
An automorphism of a group is an isomorphism of the group to itself.
An automorphism is an endomorphism that is also an isomorphism.

A ring automorphism is a ring isomorphism between the same ring; in other words, it is a unit element of the endomorphism ring of the ring that is multiplicative and preserves the multiplicative identity.
Azumaya's theorem says that two decompositions into modules with local endomorphism rings are equivalent.
An Azumaya algebra is a generalization of a central simple algebra to a non-field base ring.

glossaries: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: (B, N) pair
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: A Boolean algebra
The Baire category theorem states that a complete metric space is a Baire space; if $U_i$ is a sequence of open dense subsets, then $\cap_1^{\infty} U_i$ is dense.
René-Louis Baire
A category is balanced if every bimorphism (i.e., both mono and epi) is an isomorphism.
A subset S of a vector space over real or complex numbers is balanced if $\lambda S \subset S$ for every scalar $\lambda$ of length at most one.
A bipartite or multipartite graph is balanced if each two subsets of its vertex partition have sizes within one of each other.
balanced module
A base point is a point common to all members of a family.

A collection B of open sets is a base (or basis) for a topology $\tau$ if every open set in $\tau$ is a union of sets in $ B $. The topology $\tau$ is the smallest topology on $X$ containing $B$ and is said to be generated by $B$.
See continuous poset.
See Base.
In a vector space, a linearly independent set of vectors spanning the whole vector space.
A basis of a module $M$ is a set of elements in $M$ such that every element in the module can be expressed as a finite sum of elements in the basis in a unique way.
A pair of integers giving the degrees of a bihomogeneous polynomial in two sets of variables
An ordered pair of integers, giving the degrees of a form relative to two sets of variables.
A big line bundle L on X of dimension n is a line bundle such that $\displaystyle \limsup_{l \to \infty} \operatorname{dim} \Gamma(X, L^l) / l^n > 0$.

The word "big" when applied to a module emphasizes that the module is not necessarily finitely generated. In particular a big Cohen–Macaulay module is a module that has a system of parameters for which it is regular.
"big" usually means "not-necessarily finitely generated".
Depending on two variables, as in binary form
Depending on 2 variables. Same as bivariate.
A binary relation is a set of ordered pairs; an element x is said to be related to another element y if and only if (x, are in the set.
Having two connected components. See .

A bipartite graph is a graph whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that the vertices in one set are not connected to each other, but may be connected to vertices in the other set. Put another way, a bipartite graph is a graph with no odd cycles; equivalently, it is a graph that may be properly colored with two colors. Bipartite graphs are often written G = (U,V,E) where U and V are the subsets of vertices of each color. However, unless the graph is connected, it may not have a unique 2-coloring.
A biregular map is a regular map with regular inverse
A biregular graph is a bipartite graph in which there are only two different vertex degrees, one for each set of the vertex bipartition.
A Boolean algebra is a distributive lattice with least element 0 and greatest element 1, in which every element x has a complement ¬x, such that x ∧ ¬x = 0 and x ∨ ¬x = 1.
A Boolean algebra is a commutative ring such that all elements satisfy x2=x

entries.definition: Armand Borel (1923 – 2003), a Swiss mathematician
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: Borel conjecture.
Borel functional calculus
A Borel measure is a measure whose domain is the Borel σ-algebra.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: Émile Borel

The boundary (or frontier) of a set is the set's closure minus its interior. Equivalently, the boundary of a set is the intersection of its closure with the closure of its complement. Boundary of a set $ A $ is denoted by $ \partial A$ or $bd$ $A$.
In a graph embedding, a boundary walk is the subgraph containing all incident edges and vertices to a face.

glossaries: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: A bounded operator is a linear operator between Banach spaces for which the image of the unit ball is bounded.

A set in a metric space is bounded if it has finite diameter. Equivalently, a set is bounded if it is contained in some open ball of finite radius. A function taking values in a metric space is bounded if its image is a bounded set.
A subset $A$ of a metric space $(X,d)$ is bounded if there is some $C > 0$ such that $d(a,b) < C$ for all $a,b \in A$.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: Brauer's theorem on induced characters states that a character on a finite group is a linear combination with integer coefficients of characters induced from elementary subgroups.
The Brauer group of a field is an abelian group consisting of all equivalence classes of central simple algebras over the field.
c space.
A C* algebra is an involutive Banach algebra satisfying $\|x^* x\| = \|x^*\| \|x\|$.
Cn is an n-vertex cycle graph; see cycle.
The cardinality of the continuum

The canonical sheaf on a normal variety X of dimension n is $\omega_X = i_* \Omega_U^n$ where i is the inclusion of the smooth locus U and $\Omega_U^n$ is the sheaf of differential forms on U of degree n. If the base field has characteristic zero instead of normality, then one may replace i by a resolution of singularities.
The canonical series is the linear series of the canonical line bundle
"Canonical module" is an alternative term for a dualizing module.

A canonical form of a graph is an invariant such that two graphs have equal invariants if and only if they are isomorphic. Canonical forms may also be called canonical invariants or complete invariants, and are sometimes defined only for the graphs within a particular family of graphs. Graph canonization is the process of computing a canonical form.

A reference to a standard or choice-free presentation of some mathematical object (e.g., canonical map, canonical form, or canonical ordering). The same term can also be used more informally to refer to something "standard" or "classic". For example, one might say that Euclid's proof is the "canonical proof" of the infinitude of primes.
A canonizant is a covariant of forms.

A covariant of a form, given by the catalecticant of the penultimate emanant. It is related to the canonical form of a form. For example, the canonizant of a binary form of degree 2n–1 has degree n and order n.
A graph formed from a given graph by deleting one vertex, especially in the context of the reconstruction conjecture. See also deck, the multiset of all cards of a graph.
The cardinality of a set

entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: The mathematician after whom Cartan-Hadamard manifolds, Cartan subalgebras, and Cartan connections are named (not to be confused with his son Henri Cartan).
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: Cartan's theorems A and B.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: A catalecticant is an invariant of a binary form of degree 2n that vanishes when the form is a sum of powers of n linear forms.
An invariant vanishing on forms that are the sum of an unusually small number of powers of linear forms.
the study of Dirac operators and Dirac type operators from geometry and analysis using clifford algebras.
Categorical logic is an approach to mathematical logic that uses category theory.

A category consists of the following data #A class of objects, #For each pair of objects X, Y, a set $\operatorname{Hom}(X, Y)$, whose elements are called morphisms from X to Y, #For each triple of objects X, Y, Z, a map (called composition) #:$\circ: \operatorname{Hom}(Y, Z) \times \operatorname{Hom}(X, Y) \to \operatorname{Hom}(X, Z), \, (g, f) \mapsto g \circ f$, #For each object X, an identity morphism $\operatorname{id}_X \in \operatorname{Hom}(X, X)$ subject to the conditions: for any morphisms $f: X \to Y$, $g: Y \to Z$ and $h: Z \to W$, *$(h \circ g) \circ f = h \circ (g \circ f)$ and $\operatorname{id}_Y \circ f = f \circ \operatorname{id}_X = f$. For example, a partially ordered set can be viewed as a category: the objects are the elements of the set and for each pair of objects x, y, there is a unique morphism $x \to y$ if and only if $x \le y$; the associativity of composition means transitivity.
The category of modules over a ring is the category where the objects are all the (say) left modules over the given ring and the morphisms module homomorphisms.
The category of rings is a category where the objects are (all) the rings and where the morphisms are (all) the ring homomorphisms.
A set of first category is the same as a meager set: a set that is the union of a countable number of nowhere-dense sets, and a set of second category is a set that is not of first category.

A scheme is catenary, if all chains between two irreducible closed subschemes have the same length. Examples include virtually everything, e.g. varieties over a field, and it is hard to construct examples that are not catenary.
A ring is called catenary if all maximal chains between two prime ideals have the same length.
Named after Arthur Cayley
A contravariant.

entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: A special point associated with some geometric object
The center of a valuation (or place) is the ideal of elements of positive order.
The center of a graph is the set of vertices of minimum eccentricity.
For a subset S of a group G, the centralizer of S in G, denoted CG(S), is the subgroup of G defined by : $\mathrm{C}_G(S)=\{ g \in G \mid gs=sg \text{ for all } s \in S\}.$

The centralizer of a subset S of a ring is the subring of the ring consisting of the elements commuting with the elements of S. For example, the centralizer of the ring itself is the centre of the ring.
A special point associated with some geometric object
An element r of a ring R is central if for all x in R. The set of all central elements forms a subring of R, known as the centre of R.
A strictly increasing or decreasing sequence of prime ideals.
Synonym for walk.
A chain is a totally ordered set or a totally ordered subset of a poset. See also .
A linearly ordered subset (of a poset)

An integer associated with a projective variety, such as its degree, rank, order, class, type. In particular the Plücker characteristics of a curve are the order, class, number of nodes, number of bitangents, number of cusps, and number of inflections.
character module
A character is a one-dimensional representation.

An integer associated with a projective variety, such as its degree, rank, order, class, type. In particular the Plücker characteristics of a curve are the order, class, number of nodes, number of bitangents, number of cusps, and number of inflections.
The characteristic of a ring is a non-negative integer generating the Z-ideal of multiples of 1 that are zero.

The characteristic of the field F is the smallest positive integer n such that ; here n·1 stands for n summands . If no such n exists, we say the characteristic is zero. Every non-zero characteristic is a prime number. For example, the rational numbers, the real numbers and the p-adic numbers have characteristic 0, while the finite field Zp with p being prime has characteristic p.
See
The characteristic of a ring is the smallest positive integer n satisfying nx = 0 for all elements x of the ring, if such an n exists. Otherwise, the characteristic is 0.

glossaries: Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.definition: Let Vect(X) be the set of isomorphism classes of vector bundles on X. We can view $X \mapsto \operatorname{Vect}(X)$ as a contravariant functor from Top to Set by sending a map ƒ:X → Y to the pullback ƒ* along it. Then a characteristic class is a natural transformation from Vect to the cohomology functor H*. Explicitly, to each vector bundle E we assign a cohomology class, say, c(E). The assignment is natural in the sense that ƒ*c(E) = c(ƒ*E).

entries.definition: Claude Chevalley (1909 – 1984), a French mathematician
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: Chevalley
A line joining two points of a variety
A chord of a cycle is an edge that does not belong to the cycle, for which both endpoints belong to the cycle.

A plane conic passing through the circular points at infinity. For real projective geometry this is much the same as a circle in the usual sense, but for complex projective geometry it is different: for example, circles have underlying topological spaces given by a 2-sphere rather than a 1-sphere.
A circle graph is the intersection graph of chords of a circle.
A component of a real algebraic curve. A circuit is called even or odd depending on whether it has an even or odd number of intersections with a generic line.
A circuit may refer to a closed trail or an element of the cycle space (an Eulerian spanning subgraph). The circuit rank of a graph is the dimension of its cycle space.
Chern class.
The class of a plane curve is the number of proper tangents passing through a generic point of the plane.

A class of graphs or family of graphs is a (usually infinite) collection of graphs, often defined as the graphs having some specific property. The word "class" is used rather than "set" because, unless special restrictions are made (such as restricting the vertices to be drawn from a particular set, and defining edges to be sets of two vertices) classes of graphs are usually not sets when formalized using set theory.
The class of a contravariant or concomitant is its degree in the covariant variables. See also degree and order.
A class is a collection of sets
A class function on a group G is a function that it is constant on the conjugacy classes of G.
A class function f on a group G is a function such that $f(g) = f(h g h^{-1})$; it is a function on conjugacy classes.
The class number of a group is the number of its conjugacy classes.
The class number of a number field is the cardinality of the ideal class group of the field.

Loosely speaking, a classifying space is a space representing some contravariant functor defined on the category of spaces; for example, $BU$ is the classifying space in the sense $[-, BU]$ is the functor <math>X \mapsto \operatorname{Vect}^{\mathbb{R
The classifying space of a category C is the geometric realization of the nerve of C.

Closed subschemes of a scheme X are defined to be those occurring in the following construction. Let J be a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-ideals. The support of the quotient sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X/J$ is a closed subset Z of X and $(Z,(\mathcal{O}_X/J)|_Z)$ is a scheme called the closed subscheme defined by the quasi-coherent sheaf of ideals J. The reason the definition of closed subschemes relies on such a construction is that, unlike open subsets, a closed subset of a scheme does not have a unique structure as a subscheme.
The closed graph theorem states that a linear operator between Banach spaces is continuous (bounded) if and only if it has closed graph.
A closed neighborhood is one that includes its central vertex; see neighbourhood.

The closure of a set is the smallest closed set containing the original set. It is equal to the intersection of all closed sets which contain it. An element of the closure of a set S is a point of closure of S.
For the transitive closure of a directed graph, see transitive.
See Kuratowski closure axioms.
A closure operator on the poset P is a function C : P → P that is monotone, idempotent, and satisfies C(x) ≥ x for all x in P.

Often used synonymous with op-; for example, a colimit refers to an op-limit in the sense that it is a limit in the opposite category. But there might be a distinction; for example, an op-fibration is not the same thing as a cofibration.

This prefix has various meanings usually involving complement graphs. For instance, a cograph is a graph produced by operations that include complementation; a cocoloring is a coloring in which each vertex induces either an independent set (as in proper coloring) or a clique (as in a coloring of the complement).

entries.definition: The codimension of a prime ideal is another name for its #height.
– The codimension of a submanifold is the dimension of the ambient space minus the dimension of the submanifold.

entries.definition: If E is a ring spectrum, then the coefficient ring of it is the ring $\pi_* E$.
A complete Noetherian local ring
Irvin Cohen
Paul Cohen
A scheme is called Cohen-Macaulay if all local rings are Cohen-Macaulay. For example, regular schemes, and Spec k[x,y]/(xy) are Cohen–Macaulay, but 50px is not.

A local ring is called Cohen–Macaulay if it is Noetherian and the Krull dimension is equal to the depth. A ring is called Cohen–Macaulay if it is Noetherian and all localizations at maximal ideals are Cohen–Macaulay.
Cohen–Macaulay module
A coherent category (for now, see https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/coherent+category).
A module is called coherent if it is finitely generated and every homomorphism to it from a finitely generated module has a finitely generated kernel.
A coherent module is a finitely generated module whose finitely generated submodules are finitely presented.
A left coherent ring is a ring such that every finitely generated left ideal of it is a finitely presented module; in other words, it is coherent as a left module over itself.
Another name for "centralizer"; i.e., the commutant of a subset S of an algebra is the algebra of the elements commuting with each element of S and is denoted by $S'$.
A generalization of the determinant to arrays of dimension greater than 2.
Probably synonymous with #accessible.
A compact operator is a linear operator between Banach spaces for which the image of the unit ball is precompact.
A space is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover. Every compact space is Lindelöf and paracompact. Therefore, every compact Hausdorff space is normal. See also quasicompact.
A compact module
An element x of a poset is compact if it is way below itself, i.e. x<<x. One also says that such an x is .
A category is complete if all small limits exist.
A linear series of divisors is called complete if it is not contained in a larger linear series.
A local complete intersection ring is a Noetherian local ring whose completion is the quotient of a regular local ring by an ideal generated by a regular sequence.
A metric space is complete if every Cauchy sequence converges.

A complete graph is one in which every two vertices are adjacent: all edges that could exist are present. A complete graph with n vertices is often denoted Kn. A complete bipartite graph is one in which every two vertices on opposite sides of the partition of vertices are adjacent. A complete bipartite graph with a vertices on one side of the partition and b vertices on the other side is often denoted Ka,b. The same terminology and notation has also been extended to complete multipartite graphs, graphs in which the vertices are divided into more than two subsets and every pair of vertices in different subsets are adjacent; if the numbers of vertices in the subsets are a, b, c, ... then this graph is denoted Ka, b, c, ....
A complete system of invariants is a set of generators for the ring of invariants.
“completely reducible" is another term for "semisimple".
"Complete set" is an old term for "transitive set"

entries.definition: A multiplicative cohomology theory E is complex-oriented if the restriction map E2(CP∞) → E2(CP1) is surjective.
The completion of a module or ring M at an ideal I is the inverse limit of the modules M/InM.
completion of a module
A completion of a poset is an order-embedding of the poset in a complete lattice.

(Noun.) A line complex, a family of lines of codimension 1 in the family of all lines in some projective space, in particular a 3-dimensional family of lines in 3-dimensional projective space. See congruence.
A complex representation is a representation of G on a complex vector space. Many authors refer complex representations simply as representations.
See Connected component/Path-connected component.

A connected component of a graph is a maximal connected subgraph. The term is also used for maximal subgraphs or subsets of a graph's vertices that have some higher order of connectivity, including biconnected components, triconnected components, and strongly connected components.
Reducible (meaning having more than one irreducible component).
Not prime
A composition of morphisms in a category is part of the datum defining the category.
Jordan Hölder composition series

A (mixed) concomitant is an invariant homogeneous polynomial in the coefficients of a form, a covariant variable, and a contravariant variable. In other words it is a (tri)homogeneous polynomial on SV⊕V⊕V* for some vector space V, where SV is some symmetric power of V and V* its dual, that is invariant under the special linear group of V. In practice V often has dimension 2. The degree, class, and order of a concomitant are its degrees in the three types of variable. Concomitants are generalizations of covariants, contravariants, and invariants.
A relative invariant of GL(V) acting on the polynomials over Sn(V)⊕V⊕V*.
The conductor of an integral domain R is the annihilator of the R-module T/R, where T is the integral closure of R in its quotient field.
Conductor (class field theory)
The cone over a space X is $CX = X \times I / X \times \{0\}$. The reduced cone is obtained from the reduced cylinder $X \wedge I_+$ by collapsing the top.

A cone is a way to express the universal property of a colimit (or dually a limit). One can show that the colimit $\varinjlim$ is the left adjoint to the diagonal functor $\Delta: C \to \operatorname{Fct}(I, C)$, which sends an object X to the constant functor with value X; that is, for any X and any functor $f: I \to C$, :$\operatorname{Hom}(\varinjlim f, X) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(f, \Delta_X),$ provided the colimit in question exists. The right-hand side is then the set of cones with vertex X.
The union of the lines joining an algebraic set with a linear algebraic set. Called a point-cone, line-cone, ... if the linear set is a point, line, ...
A graph that contains a universal vertex.
An algebraic curve of degree two.
A conic is a degree 2 curve. Short for "conic section", the intersection of a cone with a plane.

The scheme is connected as a topological space. Since the connected components refine the irreducible components any irreducible scheme is connected but not vice versa. An affine scheme Spec(R) is connected iff the ring R possesses no idempotents other than 0 and 1; such a ring is also called a connected ring. Examples of connected schemes include affine space, projective space, and an example of a scheme that is not connected is Spec(k[x]×k[x])

A category is connected if, for each pair of objects x, y, there exists a finite sequence of objects zi such that $z_0 = x, z_n = y$ and either $\operatorname{Hom}(z_i, z_{i+1})$ or $\operatorname{Hom}(z_{i+1}, z_i)$ is nonempty for any i.
A graded algebra over a field k is connected if its zeroth degree piece is k.
A space is connected if it is not the union of a pair of disjoint nonempty open sets. Equivalently, a space is connected if the only clopen sets are the whole space and the empty set.

A connected graph is one in which each pair of vertices forms the endpoints of a path. Higher forms of connectivity include strong connectivity in directed graphs (for each two vertices there are paths from one to the other in both directions), k-vertex-connected graphs (removing fewer than k vertices cannot disconnect the graph), and k-edge-connected graphs (removing fewer than k edges cannot disconnect the graph).
A connected component of a space is a maximal nonempty connected subspace. Each connected component is closed, and the set of connected components of a space is a partition of that space.
Synonym for component.

entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry
Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.definition: A correspondence between a projective space and its dual.

A form in two sets of variables, one set corresponding to a vector space and the other to its dual, or in other words an element of the symmetric algebra of V⊕V* for a vector space V. Introduced by Clebsch.

A constant sheaf on a space X is a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on X such that for some set A and some map $A \to \mathcal{F}(X)$, the natural map $A \to \mathcal{F}(X) \to \mathcal{F}_x$ is bijective for any x in X.

A functor is constant if it maps every object in a category to the same object A and every morphism to the identity on A. Put in another way, a functor $f: C \to D$ is constant if it factors as: $C \to \{ A \} \overset{i}\to D$ for some object A in D, where i is the inclusion of the discrete category { A }.

For a Noetherian ring, a constructible subset of the spectrum is one that is a finite union of locally closed sets. For rings that are not Noetherian the definition of a constructible subset is more complicated.
A set is called constructible if it is in the constructible universe.

glossaries: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: Continuous cohomology.
A function from one space to another is continuous if the preimage of every open set is open.
continuous module
A function $f : X \to Y$ between metric spaces $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ is continuous if for any convergent sequence $x_n \to x$ in $X$, we have $f(x_n) \to f(x)$ in $Y$.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: Is a function for which sufficiently small changes in the input result in arbitrarily small changes in the output. Otherwise, a function is said to be a discontinuous function. A continuous function with a continuous inverse function is called a homeomorphism.
See Scott-continuous.
A space is called a continuum if it a compact, connected Hausdorff space.
The continuum is the real line or its cardinality
The contraction of an ideal is the ideal given by the inverse image of some ideal under a homomorphism of rings.

Edge contraction is an elementary operation that removes an edge from a graph while merging the two vertices that it previously joined. Vertex contraction (sometimes called vertex identification) is similar, but the two vertices are not necessarily connected by an edge. Path contraction occurs upon the set of edges in a path that contract to form a single edge between the endpoints of the path. The inverse of edge contraction is vertex splitting.

A bihomogeneous polynomial in dual variables of x, y, ... and the coefficients of some homogeneous form in x, y,... that is invariant under some group of linear transformations. In other words it is a bihomogeneous polynomial on SV⊕V for some vector space V, where SV is some symmetric power of V and V* its dual, that is invariant under the special linear group of V. In practice V often has dimension at least 3, because when it has dimension 2 these are more or less the same as covariants. The degree and class of a contravariant are its degrees in the two types of variable. Contravariants generalize invariants and are special cases of concomitants, and are in some sense dual to covariants.
A relative invariant of GL(V) acting on the polynomials over Sn(V)⊕V.
The converse graph is a synonym for the transpose graph; see transpose.
The converse <° of an order < is that in which x <° y whenever y < x.

glossaries: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: A subset K of a Riemannian manifold M is called convex if for any two points in K there is a unique shortest path connecting them which lies entirely in K, see also totally convex.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: A locally convex space is a topological vector space whose topology is generated by convex subsets.

glossaries: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: A function f on a Riemannian manifold is a convex if for any geodesic $\gamma$ the function $f\circ\gamma$ is convex. A function f is called $\lambda$-convex if for any geodesic $\gamma$ with natural parameter $t$, the function $f\circ\gamma(t)-\lambda t^2$ is convex.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: In mathematics, a real-valued function defined on an n-dimensional interval is called convex (or convex downward or concave upward) if the line segment between any two points on the graph of the function lies above or on the graph, in a Euclidean space (or more generally a vector space) of at least two dimensions. Equivalently, a function is convex if its epigraph (the set of points on or above the graph of the function) is a convex set. For a twice differentiable function of a single variable, if the second derivative is always greater than or equal to zero for its entire domain then the function is convex. Well-known examples of convex functions include the quadratic function $x^2$ and the exponential function $e^x$.

glossaries: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: A method of constructing invariants from two other invariants.
The convolution $f * g$ of two functions on a convex set is given by :$(f * g)(x) = \int f(y - x)g(y) \, dy,$ provided the integration converges.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: Two ideals are called coprime if their sum is the whole ring.
Two integers are coprime (also called relatively prime) if the only positive integer that divides them both is 1.
The core of a category is the maximal groupoid contained in the category.
A k-core is the induced subgraph formed by removing all vertices of degree less than k, and all vertices whose degree becomes less than k after earlier removals. See degeneracy.
A core model is a special sort of inner model generalizing the constructible universe

An isomorphism from a projective space to the dual of a projective space, often to the dual of itself. A correlation on the projective space of a vector space is essentially the same as a nonsingular bilinear form on the vector space, up to multiplication by constants.

A numeric measure of the strength of a linear relationship between two random variables (one can use it to quantify, for example, how shoe size and height are correlated in the population). An example is the Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient, which is found by dividing the covariance of the two variables by the product of their standard deviations. Independent variables, by definition, have a correlation of 0. A population correlation is often represented by the symbol $\rho$, and a sample correlation by $r$.
A correspondence from X to Y is an algebraic subset of X×Y
A correspondence from a set A to a set B is a subset of a Cartesian product A × ; in other words, it is a binary relation but with the specification of the ambient sets A, used in the definition.

glossaries: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry
Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: – the vector bundle of cotangent spaces on a manifold.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.definition: A space X satisfies the countable chain condition if every family of non-empty, pairswise disjoint open sets is countable.
The countable chain condition (ccc) for a poset states that every antichain is countable

A bihomogeneous polynomial in x, y, ... and the coefficients of some homogeneous form in x, y,... that is invariant under some group of linear transformations. In other words it is a bihomogeneous polynomial on SV⊕V* for some vector space V, where SV is some symmetric power of V and V* its dual, that is invariant under the special linear group of V. In practice V often has dimension 2. The degree and order of a covariant are its degrees in the two types of variable. Covariants generalize invariants and are special cases of concomitants, and are in some sense dual to contravariants
(Noun) A relative invariant of GL(V) acting on the polynomials over Sn(V)⊕V*.
A collection of subsets of a space is a cover (or covering) of that space if the union of the collection is the whole space.

A vertex cover is a set of vertices incident to every edge in a graph. An edge cover is a set of edges incident to every vertex in a graph. A set of subgraphs of a graph covers that graph if its union – taken vertex-wise and edge-wise – is equal to the graph.
An element y of a poset P is said to cover an element x of P (and is called a cover of x) if x < y and there is no element z of P such that x < z < y.

A map p: Y → X is a covering or a covering map if each point of x has a neighborhood N that is evenly covered by p; this means that the pre-image of N is a disjoint union of open sets, each of which maps to N homeomorphically.
See Cover.
A generalization of a homogeneous coordinate ring. See Cox ring.
A Cox ring is a sort of universal homogeneous coordinate ring for a projective variety.

A critical graph for a given property is a graph that has the property but such that every subgraph formed by deleting a single vertex does not have the property. For instance, a factor-critical graph is one that has a perfect matching (a 1-factor) for every vertex deletion, but (because it has an odd number of vertices) has no perfect matching itself. Compare hypo-, used for graphs which do not have a property but for which every one-vertex deletion does.
The critical point κ of an elementary embedding j is the smallest ordinal κ with (κ) > κ
Degree 3, especially a degree 3 projective variety
Cube graph, the eight-vertex graph of the vertices and edges of a cube.
(Adjective) Degree 3
An algebraic variety of dimension one.
A curve (also called a curved line in older texts) is, generally speaking, an object similar to a line but that need not be straight.
A curve together with an embedding into projective space.

glossaries: Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.definition: A cusp is a singular point of a curve whose tangent cone is a line.

entries.source_glossary: Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.definition: If X is a connected space with more than one point, then a point x of X is a cut point if the subspace X − {x} is disconnected.
See articulation point.
Given a representation $(\pi, V)$ of a Banach algebra $A$, a cyclic vector is a vector $v \in V$ such that $\pi(A)v$ is dense in $V$.
A module is called a cyclic module if it is generated by one element.
A cyclic G-module is a G-module generated by a single vector. For example, an irreducible representation is necessarily cyclic.
(Adjective) Degree 10
(Adjective) Degree 10
(Adjective) Degree 10
(Adjective) Degree 10
See tree decomposition, path decomposition, or branch-decomposition.
A decomposition of a module is a way to express a module as a direct sum of submodules.

glossaries: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: Dedekind zeta function.
A Dedekind cut is one way to construct real numbers.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: Dedekind's theorem on linear independence of characters.
Richard Dedekind
The deficiency of a linear system is its codimension in the corresponding complete linear system.

The ramification defect or ramification deficiency d of a valuation of a field K is given by [L:K]=defg where e is the ramification index, f is the inertia degree, and g is the number of extensions of the valuation to a larger field L. The number d is a power pδ of the characteristic p, and sometimes δ rather than d is called the ramification deficiency.
The degree of a line bundle L on a complete variety is an integer d such that $\chi(L^{\otimes m}) = {d \over n!} m^n + O(m^{n-1})$.
de Rham cohomology, the cohomology of complex of differential forms.
The number of intersection points of a projective variety with a generic linear subspace of complementary dimension

The degree of a vertex in a graph is its number of incident edges. The degree of a graph G (or its maximum degree) is the maximum of the degrees of its vertices, often denoted Δ(G); the minimum degree of G is the minimum of its vertex degrees, often denoted δ(G). Degree is sometimes called valency; the degree of v in G may be denoted dG(v), d(G), or deg(v). The total degree is the sum of the degrees of all vertices; by the handshaking lemma it is an even number. The degree sequence is the collection of degrees of all vertices, in sorted order from largest to smallest. In a directed graph, one may distinguish the in-degree (number of incoming edges) and out-degree (number of outgoing edges).
The degree of a form is the total power of the variables in it.
Δ(G) (using the Greek letter delta) is the maximum degree of a vertex in G, and δ(G) is the minimum degree; see degree.
A delta number is an ordinal of the form ωωα
the minimal cardinality of a dense subset of a topological space. A set of density ℵ0 is a separable space.
In a graph of n nodes, the density is the ratio of the number of edges of the graph to the number of edges in a complete graph on n nodes. See dense graph.

The I-depth (also called grade) of a module M over a ring R, where I is an ideal, is the smallest integer n such that Ext(R/I,M) is nonzero. When I is the maximal ideal of a local ring this is just called the depth of M, and if in addition M is the local ring R this is called the depth of the ring R.

The depth of a node in a rooted tree is the number of edges in the path from the root to the node. For instance, the depth of the root is 0 and the depth of any one of its adjacent nodes is 1. It is the level of a node minus one. Note, however, that some authors instead use depth as a synonym for the level of a node.
An additive homomorphism d from a ring to a module that satisfies Leibniz's rule d(ab)=ad(b)+bd(a).
A derivation of a possibly-non-associative algebra A over a commutative ring R is an R-linear endomorphism that satisfies the Leibniz rule.

glossaries: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: The derivative of a function of a real variable measures the sensitivity to change of the function value (output value) with respect to a change in its argument (input value). Derivatives are a fundamental tool of calculus. For example, the derivative of the position of a moving object with respect to time is the object's velocity: this measures how quickly the position of the object changes when time advances.
Given a map $f : E \to F$ between normed spaces, the derivative of $f$ at a point x is a (unique) linear map $T : E \to F$ such that $\lim_{h \to 0} \| f(x + h) - f(x) - Th \|/\|h\| = 0$.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: The determinant is a joint invariant of n vectors of an n-dimensional space.
The unique scalar function over square matrices which is distributive over matrix multiplication, multilinear in the rows and columns, and takes the value of $1$ for the identity matrix.
The determinant of a finite free module over a commutative ring is the r-th exterior power of the module when r is the rank of the module.
A deviation of a local ring is an invariant that measures how far the ring is from being regular.

Given a category C, a diagram in C is a functor $f: I \to C$ from a category I. For example, if $I = \mathbb{N}$ with no morphisms other than the identities, a diagram simply amounts to a sequence of objects. For a general I, typically there is a morphism between the images of objects in I under f (whence the term diagram).
A visual representation of the relationship(s) between variables.

glossaries: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: of a metric space is the supremum of distances between pairs of points.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: If (M, d) is a metric space and S is a subset of M, the diameter of S is the supremum of the distances d(x, y), where x and y range over S.

The diameter of a connected graph is the maximum length of a shortest path. That is, it is the maximum of the distances between pairs of vertices in the graph. If the graph has weights on its edges, then its weighted diameter measures path length by the sum of the edge weights along a path, while the unweighted diameter measures path length by the number of edges. For disconnected graphs, definitions vary: the diameter may be defined as infinite, or as the largest diameter of a connected component, or it may be undefined.

The term differential is used in calculus to refer to an infinitesimal (infinitely small) change in some varying quantity. For example, if x is a variable, then a change in the value of x is often denoted Δx (pronounced delta x). The differential dx represents an infinitely small change in the variable x. The idea of an infinitely small or infinitely slow change is extremely useful intuitively, and there are a number of ways to make the notion mathematically precise. Using calculus, it is possible to relate the infinitely small changes of various variables to each other mathematically using derivatives. If y is a function of x, then the differential dy of y is related to dx by the formula :$dy = \frac{dy}{dx} \,dx,$ where dy/dx denotes the derivative of y with respect to x. This formula summarizes the intuitive idea that the derivative of y with respect to x is the limit of the ratio of differences Δy/Δx as Δx becomes infinitesimal.
A differential of the first kind is a holomorphic 1-form.
A differential graded module or dg-module is a graded module with a differential.
A differential algebra is an algebra together with a derivation.

glossaries: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: same as Lipschitz constant.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: dilation (operator theory).

The dimension, by definition the maximal length of a chain of irreducible closed subschemes, is a global property. It can be seen locally if a scheme is irreducible. It depends only on the topology, not on the structure sheaf. See also Global dimension. Examples: equidimensional schemes in dimension 0: Artinian schemes, 1: algebraic curves, 2: algebraic surfaces.

The Krull dimension of a ring, often just called the dimension, is the maximal length of a chain of prime ideals, and the Krull dimension of a module is the maximal length of a chain of prime ideals containing its annihilator.
The number of elements of any basis of a vector space.
Philosophically, a direct integral is a continuous analog of a direct sum.
A direct product of a family of rings is a ring given by taking the cartesian product of the given rings and defining the algebraic operations component-wise.
A direct sum of modules is a module that is the direct sum of the underlying abelian group together with component-wise scalar multiplication.

The direct sum of representations V, W is a representation that is the direct sum $V \oplus W$ of the vector spaces together with the linear group action $\pi_{V \oplus W}(g)(v + w) = \pi_V(g)v + \pi_W(g)w$.
A category is discrete if each morphism is an identity morphism (of some object). For example, a set can be viewed as a discrete category.

An irreducible representation of a Lie group G is said to be in the discrete series if the matrix coefficients of it are all square integrable. For example, if G is compact, then every irreducible representation of it is in the discrete series.
The invariant (on the vector space of forms of degree d in n variables) which vanishes exactly when the corresponding hypersurface in Pn-1 is singular.

The discriminant of a form in n variables is the multivariate resultant of the n differentials with respect to each of the variables. For binary forms the discriminant vanishes if the form has multiple roots and is essentially the same as the discriminant of a polynomial of 1 variable. The discriminant of a form vanishes when the corresponding hypersurface has singularities (as a scheme).
See metric space.
The distance between any two vertices in a graph is the length of the shortest path having the two vertices as its endpoints.

glossaries: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: A distribution in number theory is a generalization/variant of a distribution in analysis.
A distribution is a type of a generalized function; precisely, it is a continuous linear functional on the space of test functions.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: A divisor of an integral domain is an equivalence class of non-zero fractional ideals, where two such ideals are called equivalent if they are contained in the same principal fractional ideals.

A divisor or factor of an integer n is an integer m such that there exists an integer k satisfying n = mk. Divisors can be defined in exactly the same way for polynomials or for elements of a commutative ring.
In an integral domain R, an element a is called a divisor of the element b (and we say a divides b) if there exists an element x in R with .
A domain or integral domain is a ring with no zero-divisors and where 1≠0.
Domain is a general term for objects like those that are studied in domain theory. If used, it requires further definition.
A domain is a nonzero ring with no zero divisors except 0. For a historical reason, a commutative domain is called an integral domain.

A morphism is called dominant, if the image f(X) is dense. A morphism of affine schemes Spec A → Spec B is dense if and only if the kernel of the corresponding map B → A is contained in the nilradical of B.

The irreducible representations of a simply-connected compact Lie group are indexed by their highest weight. These dominant weights form the lattice points in an orthant in the weight lattice of the Lie group.
A space $Y$ is said to dominate a space $X$ if there are $p : Y \to X$ and $g : X \to Y$ such that $p \circ g : X \to X$ is homotopic to the identity.
A local ring B is said to dominate a local ring A if it contains A and the maximal ideal of B contains the maximal ideal of A.

In mathematics, the dot product or scalar product is an algebraic operation that takes two equal-length sequences of numbers (usually coordinate vectors) and returns a single number. In Euclidean geometry, the dot product of the Cartesian coordinates of two vectors is widely used and often called "the" inner product (or rarely projection product) of Euclidean space even though it is not the only inner product that can be defined on Euclidean space; see also inner product space.
Given two vectors of the same length, the dot product is the sum of the products of their corresponding indices.
The dual of a projective space is the set of hyperplanes, considered as another projective space.
Grothendieck local duality is a duality for cohomology of modules over a local ring.
The continuous dual of a topological vector space is the vector space of all the continuous linear functionals on the space.
A dual graph of a plane graph G is a graph that has a vertex for each face of G.

For a poset (P, ≤), the dual order Pd = (P, ≥) is defined by setting x ≥ y if and only if y ≤ x. The dual order of P is sometimes denoted by Pop, and is also called opposite or converse order. Any order theoretic notion induces a dual notion, defined by applying the original statement to the order dual of a given set. This exchanges ≤ and ≥, meets and joins, zero and unit.

The dual representation (or the contragredient representation) of a representation V is a representation that is the dual vector space $V^* = \operatorname{Hom}(V, k)$ together with the linear group action that preserves the natural pairing $V^* \times V \to k$
Grothendieck local duality is a duality for cohomology of modules over a local ring.
dualizing module

The number e is a mathematical constant that is the base of the natural logarithm: the unique number whose natural logarithm is equal to one. It is approximately equal to 2.71828, and is the limit of (1 + 1/n)n as n approaches infinity, an expression that arises in the study of compound interest. It can also be calculated as the sum of the infinite series :$e = \displaystyle\sum\limits_{n = 0}^{ \infty} \dfrac{1}{n!} = \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{1\cdot 2} + \frac{1}{1\cdot 2\cdot 3} + \cdots$
E(G) is the edge set of G; see edge set.
E(X) is the membership relation of the set X

glossaries: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: An edge is (together with vertices) one of the two basic units out of which graphs are constructed. Each edge has two (or in hypergraphs, more) vertices to which it is attached, called its endpoints. Edges may be directed or undirected; undirected edges are also called lines and directed edges are also called arcs or arrows. In an undirected simple graph, an edge may be represented as the set of its vertices, and in a directed simple graph it may be represented as an ordered pair of its vertices. An edge that connects vertices x and y is sometimes written xy.
Edge-of-the-wedge theorem.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

entries.definition: An effective cycle or divisor is one with no negative coefficients
See
Named after Gotthold Eisenstein
Eisenstein series
Eisenstein series

A proof or a result is called "elementary" if it only involves basic concepts and methods in the field, and is to be contrasted with deep results which require more development within or outside the field. The concept of "elementary proof" is used specifically in number theory, where it usually refers to a proof that does not resort to methods from complex analysis.
elementary divisor
An elementary embedding is a function preserving all properties describable in the language of set theory
An elliptic curve is a smooth projective curve of genus one.
Elliptic curve
An embedded variety is one contained in a larger variety, sometimes called the ambient variety.
An embedded prime of a module is a non-minimal associated prime.

glossaries: Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.source_glossary: Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.definition: A graph embedding is a topological representation of a graph as a subset of a topological space with each vertex represented as a point, each edge represented as a curve having the endpoints of the edge as endpoints of the curve, and no other intersections between vertices or edges. A planar graph is a graph that has such an embedding onto the Euclidean plane, and a toroidal graph is a graph that has such an embedding onto a torus. The genus of a graph is the minimum possible genus of a two-dimensional manifold onto which it can be embedded.
The end of a functor <math>F: C^{\text{op
An end of an infinite graph is an equivalence class of rays, where two rays are equivalent if there is a third ray that includes infinitely many vertices from both of them.
An endomorphism is a module homomorphism from a module to itself.

An endomorphism ring is a ring formed by the endomorphisms of an object with additive structure; the multiplication is taken to be function composition, while its addition is pointwise addition of the images.
A curve tangent to a family of curves. See .
An injective envelope (or hull) of a module is a minimal injective module containing it.
A functor is an equivalence if it is faithful, full and essentially surjective.
In intersection theory, a positive-dimensional variety sometimes behaves formally as if it were a finite number of points; this number is called its equivalence.
Erdős–Kac theorem
Paul Erdős
A submodule M of N is called an essential submodule if it intersects every nonzero submodule of N.
Given a module M, an essential submodule N of M is a submodule that every nonzero submodule of M intersects non-trivially.
Localization of a finite type scheme.
An algebra is said to be essentially of finite type over another algebra if it is a localization of a finitely generated algebra.
A contravariant defined by Sylvester depending on an invariant. See .
A contravariant given by the action of an evector.
A sequence of pointed sets $X \overset{f}\to Y \overset{g}\to Z$ is exact if the image of f coincides with the pre-image of the chosen point of Z.

An exact sequence is typically a sequence (from arbitrary negative integers to arbitrary positive integers) of maps ::$\cdots \to E_1 \overset{f_1}\to E_2 \overset{f_2}\to E_3 \to \cdots$ :such that the image of $f_i$ is the kernel of $f_{i+1}$. The notion can be generalized in various ways.
exact sequence

glossaries: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras
Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

entries.definition: The exponential map for a Lie group G with $\mathfrak g$ is a map $\mathfrak g \to G$ which is not necessarily a homomorphism but satisfies a certain universal property.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: An exact sequence $0 \to \mathfrak{g}' \to \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{g} \to 0$ or $\mathfrak{g}$ is called a Lie algebra extension of $\mathfrak{g}$ by $\mathfrak{g}'$.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

entries.definition: An extension of an ideal is the ideal generated by the image under a homomorphism of rings.
Extension of scalars uses a ring homomorphism from R to S to convert R-modules to S-modules.
For partial orders ≤ and ≤′ on a set X, ≤′ is an extension of ≤ provided that for all elements x and y of X, x ≤ y implies that x ≤′ y.
A ring E is a ring extension of a ring R if R is a subring of E.
If R is a relation on a class then the extension of an element y is the class of x such that xRy
The exterior of a set is the interior of its complement.
An exterior power of a representation V is a representation $\wedge^n(V)$ with the group action induced by $V^{\otimes n} \to \wedge^n(V)$.

entries.definition: An Fσ is a union of a countable number of closed sets
A factor is a von Neumann algebra with trivial center.

A factor of a graph is a spanning subgraph: a subgraph that includes all of the vertices of the graph. The term is primarily used in the context of regular subgraphs: a k-factor is a factor that is k-regular. In particular, a 1-factor is the same thing as a perfect matching. A factor-critical graph is a graph for which deleting any one vertex produces a graph with a 1-factor.
See the entry for divisor.

A graph factorization is a partition of the edges of the graph into factors; a k-factorization is a partition into k-factors. For instance a 1-factorization is an edge coloring with the additional property that each vertex is incident to an edge of each color.
Factorization is the process of splitting a mathematical object, often integers or polynomials, into a product of factors.
A facultative point is one where a given function is positive.
A facultative point is one where a given function is positive.
A functor is faithful if it is injective when restricted to each hom-set.
A faithful module is a module whose annihilator is 0.
A linear functional $\omega$ on an involutive algebra is faithful if $\omega(x^*x) \ne 0$ for each nonzero element $x$ in the algebra.
A faithful module $M$ is one where the action of each nonzero $r \in R$ on $M$ is nontrivial (i.e. $rx \ne 0$ for some $x$ in $M$). Equivalently, $\textrm{Ann}(M)$ is the zero ideal.
A faithful representation $\pi: G \to GL(V)$ is a representation such that $\pi$ is injective as a function.

glossaries: Glossary of arithmetic and diophantine geometry

entries.definition: Fermat's Last Theorem, the most celebrated conjecture of Diophantine geometry, was proved by Andrew Wiles and Richard Taylor.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of arithmetic and diophantine geometry

entries.definition: Fermat's Last Theorem, one of the most famous and difficult to prove theorems in number theory, states that for any integer n > 2, the equation an + bn = cn has no positive integer solutions.

glossaries: Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.definition: Given $f: X \to Y$ between schemes, the fiber of f over y is, as a set, the pre-image $f^{-1}(y) = \{x \in X|f(x)=y\}$; it has the natural structure of a scheme over the residue field of y as the fiber product $X \times_Y \{y\}$, where $\{y\}$ has the natural structure of a scheme over Y as Spec of the residue field of y.
– In a fiber bundle, $\pi:E \to B$ the preimage $\pi^{-1}(x)$ of a point $x$ in the base $B$ is called the fiber over $x$, often denoted $E_x$.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.definition: Another term for the "pullback" in the category theory.

Given a category C and a set I, the fiber product over an object S of a family of objects Xi in C indexed by I is the product of the family in the slice category $C_{/S}$ of C over S (provided there are $X_i \to S$). The fiber product of two objects X and Y over an object S is denoted by $X \times_S Y$ and is also called a Cartesian square.
A commutative ring such that every nonzero element has an inverse
A field is a commutative division ring; i.e., a nonzero ring in which each nonzero element is invertible.

A field is a commutative ring (F, +, *) in which 0 ≠ 1 and every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse. In a field we thus can perform the operations addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.

See also: Filters in topology. A filter on a space X is a nonempty family F of subsets of X such that the following conditions hold: # The empty set is not in F. # The intersection of any finite number of elements of F is again in F. # If A is in F and if B contains A, then B is in F.
A subset X of a poset P is called a filter if it is a filtered upper set. The dual notion is called ideal.
A filter is a non-empty subset of a poset that is downward-directed and upwards-closed

A filtered category (also called a filtrant category) is a nonempty category with the properties (1) given objects i and j, there are an object k and morphisms i → k and j → k and (2) given morphisms u, v: i → j, there are an object k and a morphism w: j → k such that w ∘ u = w ∘ v. A category I is filtered if and only if, for each finite category J and functor f: J → I, the set $\varprojlim \operatorname{Hom}(f(j), i)$ is nonempty for some object i in I.
A non-empty subset X of a poset P is called filtered, if, for all elements x and y of X, there is an element z of X such that z ≤ x and z ≤ y. The dual notion is called directed.

One of Grothendieck's fundamental ideas is to emphasize relative notions, i.e. conditions on morphisms rather than conditions on schemes themselves. The category of schemes has a final object, the spectrum of the ring $ \mathbb{Z} $ of integers; so that any scheme $ S $ is over $ \textrm{Spec} (\mathbb{Z}) $, and in a unique way.
Synonymous with terminal

The morphism is finite if $X$ may be covered by affine open sets $ \text{Spec }B $ such that each $ f^{-1}(\text{Spec }B) $ is affine — say of the form $ \text{Spec }A $ — and furthermore $ A $ is finitely generated as a $ B $-module. See finite morphism. Finite morphisms are quasi-finite, but not all morphisms having finite fibers are quasi-finite, and morphisms of finite type are usually not quasi-finite.
A category is finite if it has only finitely many morphisms.
A finite module (or algebra) over a ring usually means one that is finitely generated as a module. It may also mean one with a finite number of elements, especially in the term finite field.

A graph is finite if it has a finite number of vertices and a finite number of edges. Many sources assume that all graphs are finite without explicitly saying so. A graph is locally finite if each vertex has a finite number of incident edges. An infinite graph is a graph that is not finite: it has infinitely many vertices, infinitely many edges, or both.

When said of the value of a variable assuming values from the non-negative extended reals $\R_{\geq 0}\cup\{\infty\},$ the meaning is usually "not infinite". For example, if the variance of a random variable is said to be finite, this implies it is a non-negative real number, possibly zero. In some contexts though, for example in "a small but finite amplitude", zero and infinitesimals are meant to be excluded. When said of the value of a variable assuming values from the extended natural numbers $\N\cup\{\infty\},$ the meaning is simply "not infinite". When said of a set or a mathematical object whose main component is a set, it means that the cardinality of the set is less than $\aleph_0$.
The term "finite module" is another name for a finitely generated module.

If y is a point of Y, then the morphism f is of finite presentation at y (or finitely presented at y) if there is an open affine neighborhood U of f(y) and an open affine neighbourhood V of y such that f(V) ⊆ U and $\mathcal{O}_Y(V)$ is a finitely presented algebra over $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$. The morphism f is locally of finite presentation if it is finitely presented at all points of Y. If X is locally Noetherian, then f is locally of finite presentation if, and only if, it is locally of finite type. The morphism is of finite presentation (or Y is finitely presented over X) if it is locally of finite presentation, quasi-compact, and quasi-separated. If X is locally Noetherian, then f is of finite presentation if, and only if, it is of finite type.
A finite free presentation of a module M is an exact sequence $F_1 \to F_0 \to M$ where $F_i$ are finitely generated free modules.

The morphism is locally of finite type if $ X $ may be covered by affine open sets $ \text{Spec }B $ such that each inverse image $f^{-1}(\text{Spec }B)$ is covered by affine open sets $\text{Spec }A$ where each $ A $ is finitely generated as a $B$-algebra. The morphism is of finite type if $ X $ may be covered by affine open sets $ \text{Spec }B $ such that each inverse image $f^{-1}(\text{Spec }B)$ is covered by finitely many affine open sets $\text{Spec }A$ where each $ A $ is finitely generated as a $B$-algebra.
An algebra over a ring is said to be of finite type if it is finitely generated as an algebra.

A module over a ring is called finitely generated if every element is a linear combination of a fixed finite number of elements. If the module happens to be an algebra this is much stronger than saying it is finitely generated as an algebra.
See Alexandrov topology.

A module $M$ is finitely generated if there exist finitely many elements $x_1,...,x_n$ in $M$ such that every element of $M$ is a finite linear combination of those elements with coefficients from the scalar ring $R$.

A left ideal I is finitely generated if there exist finitely many elements such that . A right ideal I is finitely generated if there exist finitely many elements such that . A two-sided ideal I is finitely generated if there exist finitely many elements such that .

A morphism $ f $ is flat if it gives rise to a flat map on stalks. When viewing a morphism as a family of schemes parametrized by the points of $ X $, the geometric meaning of flatness could roughly be described by saying that the fibers $f^{-1}(x)$ do not vary too wildly.
(Noun) A linear subspace of projective space, such as a point, line, plane, hyperplane.
A flat module is a module such that tensoring with it preserves exactness.
A $R$-module $F$ is called a flat module if the tensor product functor $- \otimes_R F$ is exact.In particular, every projective module is flat.
A homogeneous polynomial in several variables. Same as quantic.
A homogeneous polynomial in several variables, also called a quantic.
See formal scheme.
Qualifies anything that is sufficiently precise to be translated straightforwardly in a formal system. For example. a formal proof, a formal definition.

glossaries: Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.definition: – A frame at a point of a differentiable manifold M is a basis of the tangent space at the point.
entries.source_glossary: Glossary of differential geometry and topology

entries.definition: A frame F is a complete lattice, in which, for every x in F and every subset Y of F, the infinite distributive law x ∧ $\bigvee$Y = $\bigvee${x ∧ y | y in Y} holds. Frames are also known as locales and as complete Heyting algebras.

Synonymous with unbased. For example, the free path space of a space X refers to the space of all maps from I to X; i.e., $X^I$ while the path space of a based space X consists of such map that preserve the base point (i.e., 0 goes to the base point of X).
A free module is a module that has a basis, or equivalently, one that is isomorphic to a direct sum of copies of the scalar ring $R$.
A free ideal ring or a fir is a ring in which every right ideal is a free module of fixed rank.
Frobenius reciprocity.

The Frobenius reciprocity states that for each representation $\sigma$ of H and representation $\pi$ of G there is a bijection :$\operatorname{Hom}_G(\pi, \operatorname{Ind}_H^G \sigma) = \operatorname{Hom}_H(\pi|_H, \sigma)$ that is natural in the sense that $\operatorname{Ind}_H^G$ is the right adjoint functor to the restriction functor $\pi \mapsto \pi|_H$.
A Fréchet space is a topological vector space whose topology is given by a countable family of seminorms (which makes it a metric space) and that is complete as a metric space.
See T1.
A functor is full if it is surjective when restricted to each hom-set.
Synonym for induced.

Is a process or a relation that associates each element x of a set X, the domain of the function, to a single element y of another set Y (possibly the same set), the codomain of the function. If the function is called f, this relation is denoted y = f (read f of x), the element x is the argument or input of the function, and y is the value of the function, the output, or the image of x by f. The symbol that is used for representing the input is the variable of the function (one often says that f is a function of the variable x).

A function is an ordered triple (A, B, f consisting of sets A, B and a subset f of the Cartesian product A × B subject to the condition (a, b), (a, b′) ∈ f implies b . In other words, it is a special kind of correspondence where given an element a of A, there is a unique element b of B that corresponds to it.
